text: This is a sixty-three-year-old female who presented with recurrent right iliac fossa pain, colicky in nature for the last one week. No other comorbidities and no previous surgeries. Ultrasound abdomen was showing a mass colonic in origin in the right iliac fossa. Colonoscopy was showing an ulceroproliferative lesion at the IC junction. Biopsy showing moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. CEA report was CCT abdomen was showing a polypoidal mass at the IC area with irregular bowel wall thickening, probably neoplastic in origin. So we did a, planning was a lap right radical hemicolectomy and patient position was a leg split with the surgeon standing in between the legs as well as on the right side depending upon the area of surgery. And you proceed with a five port technique where you use the camera port either in the umbilicus or in the suprapubic area and five working ports and the right side working ports both as right hand and left hand working port depending upon the infracolic and supracolic dissection.

The surgeon performs the IRETA technique, initially dissecting the mesocolon from the retroperitoneum through an avascular plane to protect the ureter and gonadal vessels. The avascular plane is visible between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum. The duodenum is encountered superiorly and medially, and the surgeon carefully separates it from the mesocolon, revealing Gerota's fascia adjacent to the duodenum. After mobilizing the duodenum, the pancreas becomes visible, and the surgeon continues to dissect the duodenum-pancreatic complex from the mesocolon. The colon is reflected after mobilizing this plane.

The surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric vein with draining vessels at the head of the pancreas. The intracolic dissection is paused, and the focus shifts to the supracolic area. The gastrocolic omentum is opened to access the lesser sac, releasing adhesions from the pancreas and stomach. A clear plane between the mesocolon and stomach is visible, with mesocolic tissues being separated. The surgeon carefully dissects between the mesocolonic vasculature and stomach tissue, ensuring the colonic vasculature is safeguarded. A gauze is placed to protect the underlying duodenum, facilitating communication between the infracolic and supracolic dissection planes.

The surgeon removes the final fibers of fatty tissue from the head of the pancreas, revealing the superior mesenteric vein and its draining veins. Dissection continues up to the root of the superior mesenteric vein, entering the 'tunnel of love.' The middle colic vein is visible draining into the superior mesenteric vein, and the right colic vein is identified, clipped, and divided. A gauze is placed for later identification. The procedure shifts to the infracolic compartment, focusing on central vascular ligation. The superior mesenteric vein is clearly visible, and the surgeon marks the left side of the vein with a unipolar cautery. Dissection begins below the ileocolic vessels, with traction applied to these vessels. Due to cecal growth, fibro-fatty tissue around the ileocecal root is removed, marked four centimeters from the root. The surgeon carefully dissects into the adventitial layer of the superior mesenteric vein, ensuring tissues to the left of the vein are included in the specimen.

The surgeon clips the ileocolic vein as it joins the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic artery is dissected up to its root, and vessels are clipped at the origin. Dissection continues on the surface of the superior mesenteric artery to identify its branches, including the middle colic artery and a small right colic artery arising from it, which is clipped and divided.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic vein and dissects tissues from the root of the middle colic artery to its bifurcation into the right and left branches. The right branch of the middle colic artery is divided due to the tumor's location at the sacral area. The omentum is separated from the proposed transaction area of the transverse colon. The lateral attachments of the ascending colon are separated, and the terminal ileum is divided at least ten centimeters away from the ileocecal valve. Adhesions of the cecum to the abdominal wall are released. The specimen is extracted through a small midline incision near the umbilicus, followed by extracorporeal ileotransverse anastomosis.
